Fig. 15. Changelogs missing records of some patched versions.

For example, the maximum committed version of CVE-2020- 12655 in the changelog is V 5 . 4 . 50 , but the actual maximum patched version is V 5 . 7 . Therefore, there was a missing record of the corresponding changelog. As shown in Fig. 15 , we found 94 versions have been patched, but their commit-ids were not recorded in the corresponding changelogs. In particular, the changelogs released in 2016 had the highest error rate 16% (36 out of 221 CVE-IDs released in 2016), followed by 6% in 2018 (20 out of 338 CVE-IDs released in 2018).
Overall, for the 1,367 Linux kernel CVE-IDs, the number of

false positives for the upto versions of the NVD reached 276, and the number of false negatives reached 289, accounting for 20.19% and 21.14%, respectively. As shown in Figs. 13 and 14 , we show the error rates of upto versions of the NVD and the error rates of all vulnerable version sets of the NVD over the years, respectively. Corresponding to the CVE-IDs, the denominator of the formula for calculating the rates is the total number of vulnerable versions reported by the NVD in one year, and the numerator is the number of false positives or false negatives for those versions. Among the 1,367 CVE-IDs, the total number of the vulnerable versions reported by NVD was 43,727, of which the total number of false positives reached 2,497, and the total number of false negatives reached 9,330, accounting for 5.7% and 21.34%, respectively.
E. Comparison With ReDeBug and VUDDY
In this section, we will compare with two well-known vulner-
able code detection tools that are available, ReDeBug [32] and
Fig. 16. In our ﬁve datasets, the number of repatch we found.

VUDDY [43] . ReDeBug uses a syntax-based pattern matching approach to detect unpatched code clones in OS-distribution scale code bases, and VUDDY uses coarse-grained function feature values to detect code clones. Our vulnerability database includes Linux kernel (totally 1,367 CVE-IDs, 2,664 security patches by Sep. 2020), OpenSSL (totally 91 CVE-IDs, 165 patches by Jan. 2021), BusyBox (totally 13 CVE-IDs, 20 patches by Jan.2021), Wget (totally 22 CVE-IDs, 31 patches by Jan. 2021), QEMU (totally 114 CVE-IDs, 145 patches by Jan.2021).
To measure and compare the accuracy of the three vulnera-

bility detection methods, we randomly select one version from each of the above ﬁve pieces of software, as shown in the ﬁrst column of Table III . We check each version against all the 2,576 CVE-IDs. With the ground truth (in total 69 vulnerabilities) established by the developer logs (i.e., changelogs and git logs), we show the accuracy of the three methods in Table III . One can observe that neither ReDeBug [32] nor VUDDY [43] could achieve high precision and recall here, whereas our method has 94.3%precisionand95.6%recall. SomeCVEsreleasedbyNVD do not have corresponding patch(es), and these CVEs slipped out from the detection of our tool, which is the reason for the 95.6% recall of our tool. As shown in Fig. 16 , under these ﬁve data sets, weshowthenumberofrepatchescollectedbyyear.Aftermanual veriﬁcation,itisfoundthatsomepartiallymatchedcodereported by our tool do not have the CVE vulnerability, which is also the reason for the 94.3% precision of our tool. We summarized the reasons that led to the false positives and false negatives with ReDebug and VUDDY: (1) they wrongly identiﬁed repatched code (i.e., repatched in a different way) as a vulnerability, and (2) some exact or partial matching vulnerable code were not identiﬁed. Further, ReDebug detects by comparing the similarity between patch and vulnerability code, and the granularity is coarse, resulting in a large number of false positives. VUDDY is designed to achieve low false positive rates, which appear to be inherent to the approach of detecting vulnerabilities caused by code clones. However, when using this approach to detecting vulnerabilities that are not caused by code clones, high false negative rates occur. Note that although here the measurement results are not based on all versions with all CVE-IDs, it is sufﬁcient to demonstrate the relative performance of different methods and the weaknesses of the two existing methods.
